nuggets.summary: In Starbucks's early days in Vancouver, Behar had a hugely profitable roughly 900-square-foot store on a high-traffic corner (Robson and Thurlow), but the lease had a demolition clause letting the landlord evict them at any time. Instead of waiting to be forced out, Behar had the company's real estate lead secure a second location directly kitty-corner from the first, in a heritage building, without any answer for what would happen if the new store simply cannibalized the old one's sales. When the second store opened, it doubled total sales volume on the corner instead of splitting it, because the two nearly-adjacent locations pulled in completely different customer types -- 'the suits' at one and 'the bikers' at the other. That result contradicted the conventional retail instinct to avoid clustering stores so close together for fear of cannibalization, and it became the basis for Starbucks's later strategy of opening multiple stores close together in dense urban corridors.
nuggets.quote: So anyway, I thought it'd take her a couple of years. It took her about six months. And she came back and said, we got the building. So I said, okay, let's take it. So we opened the store, kitty corner from the first end. You can throw a football from one to the other... The day that we opened that store, we doubled the volume on the corner. We did exactly what we were doing on the other corner in the new store. Only one, that, the new one was the suits and the old one was the bikers.

nuggets.summary: Starbucks's pre-IPO leadership was three men internally nicknamed 'H2O': Howard Schultz, Howard Behar, and Warren Smith. Behar says the group's actual decision-making dynamic was shaped directly by their personal histories -- Schultz, the youngest at 33 and hungry to build something big, kept pushing for speed, while Behar (44, son of Depression-era immigrant parents) and Smith (48, raised by a single mother of five in Chehalis) had both 'hit rocks in the river' earlier in life and repeatedly pulled him back toward a more methodical pace. This wasn't abstract personality description -- it was the literal negotiating dynamic inside the executive team during the company's fastest growth years, with the two older, working-class-raised partners acting as a governor on a younger, ambition-driven founder, a detail that doesn't appear in the public Starbucks growth narrative.
nuggets.quote: Howard wants to be, wanted to be a star... Warren Smith was raised in Chehalis... a single mother ahead of household. Five kids... I was raised by two Depression-era parents... Howard was pushing real fast through all that stuff. And Warren and I were saying, okay, but let's not blow up... I was 44, Howard was 33, and Warren was 48.

nuggets.summary: In Starbucks's early years the stores had almost no seating -- just four or five bar stools in the window -- because the company saw itself as a transactional coffee-bean retailer that wanted customers 'in and out,' not lingering. Behar says the leadership team went 'kicking and screaming' into adding real chairs, resisting the change even as customers kept asking for it, because it meant altering the business model they'd built. The shift was triggered partly by the book The Great Good Place (about the value of a 'third place' between home and work) and by observing a University District coffee bar, the Allegro, that already had seating and regulars who sat and talked. Once Starbucks added chairs, the stores stopped being just a coffee-and-bean retailer and became a community gathering spot -- the 'third place' positioning that later defined the brand -- and Behar is explicit that this change, not the beverage business alone, is what turned Starbucks into a place people returned to daily.
nuggets.quote: So we started adding chairs. And we didn't want to do that. Sure. Because it changed the business model. But our customers were asking. But we went kicking and screaming into that business. And that changed the business. Yeah. Now we became community... The Great Good Place was about community or about the third place. And that drove the business. That was the change.

nuggets.summary: By the time Starbucks had around 200 stores, growth had made the leadership team lose track of why the company existed -- opening another store had stopped meaning anything to Behar personally. To fix that, they brought in a young academic consultant, Jim Collins, before he published any of his books, who pushed them to articulate a goal bigger than store count. The group -- which explicitly included baristas, store managers, officers and directors, not just executives -- spent a long time landing on: 'We want to be one of the most well-known and respected organizations in the world... known for nurturing and inspiring people's spirit.' Behar says the first half of that statement was almost a joke at 200 stores, but the second half genuinely changed him personally: he rewrote his own individual mission statement to match it, and says it has driven the rest of his life and leadership philosophy ever since.
nuggets.quote: We want to be one of the most well-known and respected organizations in the world... to be known for nurturing and inspiring people's spirit... That touched my soul and touched everybody's soul... Words matter. Words matter... And that drove us.

nuggets.summary: As Starbucks's store count kept escalating -- first a target of 50 new stores that seemed impossible, then 75, then past 200 -- the pressure of pure growth caused the company to lose sight of its purpose, and people were leaving who shouldn't have left. It wasn't an executive who first flagged this: Jennifer Kerriman, one of the earliest employees Schultz had hired into a fairly junior role, went directly to Behar and said 'we're losing our way,' asking what their purpose actually was beyond opening more stores. Behar calls her 'a canary in the mineship' and says he took her warning seriously enough to escalate it to Schultz and bring in outside help (eventually Jim Collins) to re-anchor the company around a cause rather than a store count. The specific lesson is which signal Behar chose to trust as the trigger for a company-defining strategic reset -- an early, non-executive employee's discomfort, not a board member or a metric.
nuggets.quote: There was a woman named Jennifer Kerriman... Jennifer came to me and she said, 'You know, we're losing our way.'... 'What's our purpose here? Why are we here? Are we just here to open stores?'... 'We've got to find our way back to our core, our cause, our reason for being.' And she was a canary in the mineship.

nuggets.summary: The idea for what became the Frappuccino came from a Southern California district manager, Dina Campion, who took Behar to visit a competitor selling a blended-ice drink and told him roughly 30 customers a day were asking Starbucks stores for something similar. Behar, without a college degree but with retail instincts, ran the math on the spot: 30 drinks a day at $3 each was about $90/day, roughly $30,000/year against a typical Starbucks store's ~$600,000 annual revenue -- a 5% sales lift from one product, which he recognized immediately as a big deal. After being told no by the head of product development, he had Campion's team (a store manager and barista who'd rigged up a blender with chocolate and no-fat milk) test the drink covertly in a single store -- no signage, no telling anyone, nightly phone updates only to Behar's home line -- and watched daily volume climb from 40 to 50 to 70 drinks per store per day within three weeks, which is what gave him the ammunition to force the idea back up the chain.
nuggets.quote: howard we've got about 30 people a day come in our store asking for a product like nice money well i didn't have a college degree but i knew retail numbers and i said 30 drinks a day there are three bucks that's 90 bucks a day that's about 600 bucks a week... i said geez that's a five percent lift in sales... i said okay you can try it but no signage and do not tell a soul... the first week 40 drinks a day per store the third second week 50 drinks a day per store the third week 70 drinks a day per store

nuggets.summary: When Behar finally brought the blended-drink test back to headquarters with real numbers, the head of product development -- a Pepsi-trained Harvard MBA -- stood up, pointed a finger in Behar's face in front of Howard Schultz, and said he'd already told them not to do this and Behar needed to stop it immediately. Rather than back down, Behar had a one-page sheet of the actual numbers (a projected 10-15% lift in sales and roughly a 20% lift in profits) and asked Schultz directly for 90 days to prove it before killing it. Schultz agreed, but set a personal, almost arbitrary veto condition -- if he himself didn't like the smell of it after 90 days, it was dead regardless of the numbers. That a company-defining product survived only because one operations exec extracted a 90-day reprieve from the founder, over the explicit objection of the official product-development lead, is the kind of internal near-death moment that doesn't appear in the polished corporate history.
nuggets.quote: george got up out of the seat and he came over to me and pointed his mirror right at my nose he said be hard i am in charge of product development i told you they weren't going to do this you stop this right now... i said howard give me 90 days if you don't like it by then i'll get rid of it... he said okay howard you got 90 days but if i just don't like the smell it's gone

nuggets.summary: The blended coffee drink that Behar fought to keep alive against his own product-development chief's explicit veto was rolled out under the name Frappuccino -- borrowed from a Boston coffee shop's name for a similar drink -- into about 100 stores as a first test at scale. Behar states that the drink went on to generate roughly $4 billion in global annual sales for Starbucks, and that at its peak it represented about 20% of the company's total sales, an enormous share of company revenue traced back to a single covert, unauthorized in-store test that nearly got shut down by headquarters.
nuggets.quote: we rolled it out into about 100 stores... and the thing was a blowout success and we called it frappuccino... global sales are about four billion dollars they had at one time were 20 of all sales and starbucks

nuggets.summary: Early in his tenure, Behar pushed to raise Starbucks store wages to a dollar above minimum wage, projecting the cost impact would be a modest roughly one percentage point hit to the P&L. When the actual numbers came in, the real cost was double what he'd projected -- about two percentage points, not one -- a meaningful miscalculation for a thin-margin retail business scaling fast. He frames this not as a minor rounding error but as a genuine misjudgment of cost, and ties it back to a broader self-assessment that his biggest career failure wasn't any single number but the lack of self-belief that made these kinds of decisions harder than they needed to be.
nuggets.quote: i wanted to raise wages... i said we got to be a dollar above minimum wage and i misjudged with the cost we did all the numbers and it turned out i thought it was going to be one point one percentage point turned out to be two it was double what was going to cost

nuggets.summary: When Howard Schultz recruited Behar to run operations at what was then a roughly dozen-store Starbucks, Behar was making $300,000 a year running a real estate company's retail division. Schultz told him the most he could pay was $100,000 a year -- a two-thirds pay cut -- so Behar negotiated for equity instead, specifically asking for 200,000 shares of stock options (non-qualified options, or 'NQs,' under the tax rules of that era). This wasn't a symbolic ask: Behar had already lost roughly a million dollars of his own savings in a previous venture and was deliberately trading salary for meaningful ownership, a specific negotiation detail most retellings of the Starbucks story compress into 'he joined as an early operator.'
nuggets.quote: I was making $300,000 a year at the real estate company. And I asked Howard, "What can you afford?" They said, "The most I can pay is $100,000 a year." I said, "Okay, but here's what I want. I want equity." I want 200,000 shares of stock options.

nuggets.summary: To fund his family's move after joining Starbucks, Behar sold his Mercer Island house and walked away with about $135,000 in equity. Rather than sit on that cash, he put $35,000 down on a new house in the same school district (to accommodate a teenage daughter who suddenly wanted to move in with him) and put another $35,000 directly into Starbucks stock, which at the time was trading at roughly a dollar a share, pre-split. It's a concrete, personal illustration of how much of his own limited net worth he was willing to concentrate in the company at a point when its future was still completely unproven.
nuggets.quote: So I put $35,000 up and down on a $135,000 house. Can you imagine that? With a view of what we're looking to live. $35,000... And I put another $35,000 in the Starbucks stock. It was a dollar share.

nuggets.summary: Starbucks funded its rapid store expansion almost entirely from its own operating cash flow rather than debt -- Behar says new stores paid back their build cost in about a year in the early days, stretching to about two years as the chain grew, and that the only times the company borrowed money were when it didn't actually need to, mainly to establish banking relationships. Behar's own criterion for approving new stores was deliberately simple and explicitly rejected the standard corporate-finance toolkit his MBA-trained colleagues favored: rather than discounted cash flow or net-present-value modeling, which he dismissed as 'made up' and 'fictitious,' his only question was what a store cost to build and how fast it paid that back in cash. For a company that grew to thousands of stores, financing that growth almost entirely off same-year store cash flow -- instead of leaning on external capital the way most fast-growing retail chains do -- is a specific, non-obvious operating fact about how Starbucks scaled.
nuggets.quote: we were paying back our stores in a year and then when it really got bad it was two years that's a credible return... we hardly we never really borrowed any money... the only time we borrowed money was when we didn't need it... i had one equation i said tell me what it cost to build the store tell me how fast we get our cash back... they're all out there using some fictitious numbers doing get present value i said what that is that's made up stuff

nuggets.summary: Behar kept a sign in his office depicting an inverted org chart: leadership at the bottom point of a triangle, with the people it serves above. Unlike the conventional 'customer is king' framing that most service businesses use to justify putting the customer at the very top, Behar's version placed Starbucks employees ('partners') above customers in the hierarchy of who leadership exists to serve -- customers came second, after the people doing the work. This ordering directly contradicts the standard retail-industry assumption that the customer relationship is the top priority leadership organizes around, and Behar treats getting that sequence right as foundational to why Starbucks's culture worked.
nuggets.quote: leadership is at the bottom of the triangle. Right? We're at the point. And the triangle goes like this. And at the top of the triangle, the human beings we're serving. And at the top of the triangle, to me, were the employees, what we call partners at Starbucks. And then those customers, those human beings we call customers.

nuggets.summary: After a Starbucks connection put Behar in touch with early Facebook -- through his son's best friend, one of Instagram's first investors -- Facebook's head of HR and two engineers asked him directly how to build a great culture. Before answering, Behar asked them what business they were in; they answered 'the technology business.' He pushed back, pointing out that a company literally named 'Facebook,' built around people connecting with each other, describing itself as a technology business rather than a people business, was itself the root of their culture problem. It's a specific, dateable consulting exchange -- not generic 'culture matters' advice -- where Behar diagnosed an early structural blind spot in how Facebook's own leadership defined its business, using the same 'what business are we really in' test he'd used inside Starbucks decades earlier.
nuggets.quote: so they said well how do you create a great culture i said well i have a question before i answer that question tell me what business you're in they said we're in the technology business... i said aren't you about people no no we're about technology that's been the story that's got them off track right they're in the people business

nuggets.summary: In his mid-20s, newly promoted to vice president at a furniture company, Behar's CEO pulled him aside and told him that to be a great executive he needed to stop 'wearing his heart on his sleeve' and stop volunteering his opinion the instant he had one. Taking this literally, Behar cycled through a series of increasingly strange self-management devices to physically stop himself from reacting in meetings: sitting on his hands, pulling paper clips out of a box one at a time, tearing the corners off legal-pad paper, and finally mimeographing small cards that read 'Howard, shut up' to keep in front of him. Within about two months of trying to suppress his own personality this way, he went from loving his job to being genuinely depressed, and had actually written a resignation letter before a colleague talked him out of quitting and reframed the CEO's advice as generational rather than a verdict on his fitness for the role. The episode is a concrete, granular account of how literally he interpreted vague executive-presence feedback, and how close it came to ending his career trajectory before Starbucks.
nuggets.quote: i took i took a box of paper books and i'd have this box of 25 or 50 paper clips i'd open the box take a paper clip out and i'd go grab another one all in an attempt to manage my behavior... i started tearing off the corners... finally i figured out the way to really do it i bimby graphed up... i typed on them howard shut up before everything... i went from a guy in about two minutes period of time a guy that loved his job to a guy that hated himself i literally got depressed

nuggets.summary: Behar describes himself as 'the worst investor ever' because he invests emotionally, based on belief in people rather than numbers. As a specific example, he says that when his lifelong friend Jeff Brotman co-founded Costco, other friends of his invested early and he passed, reasoning from his retail background that a business running on roughly 8% margins couldn't possibly make money -- a judgment that turned out to be badly wrong given Costco's eventual success on a thin-margin, membership-fee model. This is a specific instance of his deep retail-industry expertise actively working against him: the same operating intuition that made him good at running Starbucks stores led him to reject a business model that ran counter to what 'worked' in his own experience.
nuggets.quote: when costco came along i had friends that invested in costco and i said you're going to make money at 8% margin no way i was in retail i didn't understand it you know so i don't do that well i still don't do that

nuggets.summary: Although Behar's entire leadership philosophy rests on the idea that leadership means serving and caring for people, he explicitly says he doesn't think that's what separates great business builders. He specifically names Steve Jobs and Bill Gates as men he considers to have been 'shitty,' abusive leaders of people, while also crediting Jobs's specific brilliance -- an ability to 'see around corners' and spot products nobody else had identified yet -- as the real driver of Apple's success, independent of how he treated people. Coming from someone whose entire personal brand is people-first servant leadership, this is a notable break from his own stated framework: he separates the skill of building a great product or company from the separate question of whether someone is a good leader of people, rather than insisting the two must go together.
nuggets.quote: i look at merchants see around corners they find something that nobody's ever seen before in terms of product or services and they bring it to market steve jobs that was what his brilliance that was his prayers because he was a shitty leader abusive leader you know and so was bill gates a shitty leader abused people right

nuggets.summary: Behar's stated hiring philosophy is to prioritize whether someone has people skills over raw talent, but he offers a specific counter-case: a head of marketing Starbucks hired who was, in his words, the worst 'people guy,' constantly blowing up at coworkers. Rather than fire him outright given his genuine skill at solving problems creatively, the team's practical solution was to physically isolate him -- putting him in a room by himself and feeding him problems (Behar jokes about literally passing food through a door slot) so his output could be used without his interpersonal behavior damaging the rest of the team. It's a concrete illustration of how the 'hire for people-skills first' rule got bent in practice for someone whose individual skill was valuable enough to accommodate, rather than applied as an absolute filter.
nuggets.quote: we have this guy that came into head marketing he was the worst people guy with our men he would blow everybody out finally he stuck in a room by himself he was great at solving problems and really creative every morning we had a door on the window we throw in 10 pounds of broth filet mignon

nuggets.summary: When Behar was eight years old, working the register at his father's small Seattle grocery store, he watched his father hand a customer two baskets of strawberries and let them leave without ringing up the sale on the hand-cranked cash register. When young Howard pointed out the missed charge, his father told him that not everything in life needs to be paid for -- he knew this particular family loved fresh fruit and was going through a hard stretch, and treated them as neighbors and friends rather than a transaction. Behar says this single specific childhood memory, more than any formal training, is the root of his lifelong belief that serving people has to come before extracting value from them, a belief that later became central to how he ran Starbucks.
nuggets.quote: he said, Howard, not everything we do in life do we need to get paid. I said, some things in life you do because you care about people. And I happen to know that these people love strawberries and fresh fruit. And I also happen to know they're having tough times right now. And they're not our customers. They're our neighbors and our friends.

nuggets.summary: At 21, in his first management job at a furniture store in Salem, Oregon, Behar noticed his boss Sid Shetter would personally bend down to pick up cigarette butts and trash from the parking lot before doing his weekly store visits, which embarrassed Behar. When Behar told him not to bother, Shetter replied, 'If not me, who? If not now, what?' -- a line Behar says has stayed with him for the rest of his life and turned into a literal, ongoing personal habit: decades later he still won't go to bed without having picked up at least one piece of litter that day, and he'll stop his car specifically to pick up a discarded Starbucks cup (though he says he'll let a McDonald's cup go). It's a specific, durable behavioral tic traceable to one exact sentence from one specific boss, illustrating how his 'nurture and inspire' philosophy operates at a level far smaller than corporate strategy.
nuggets.quote: And he said to me, Howard, if not me, who, if not now, what?... I can't. If I walk down the street and there's a piece of paper, I don't go to bed at night without picking up one piece of garbage off the street... I can be driving down the street and see a Starbucks cup. And I'll stop in the car.
